title: Menu Engineering: What to Add, What to Kill, What to Push
subtitle: Your menu is not a list of things you can make. It is a strategic tool that drives revenue, margins, and customer satisfaction.

metrics.label: cold drink share in summer months

sections.heading: What Menu Engineering Actually Means
sections.body:
Menu engineering is the practice of analysing your menu items by two dimensions — profitability and popularity — and making strategic decisions about what to promote, improve, reprice, or remove.

Most cafe owners build their menu based on what they can make, what they like, or what they see other cafes doing. Menu engineering replaces instinct with data. It asks: which items are making you money, and which are just taking up space?

To engineer your menu, you need two numbers for every item:

**1. Contribution margin.** This is the selling price minus the food or drink cost. A flat white that sells for three pounds fifty and costs eighty pence in ingredients has a contribution margin of two pounds seventy. A chicken sandwich that sells for six pounds and costs two pounds seventy in ingredients has a contribution margin of three pounds thirty. The sandwich has a higher contribution margin but a lower percentage margin. Both numbers matter.

**2. Sales volume.** How many of each item do you sell per week? This is your popularity metric. Your POS system should give you this data. If it does not, run a manual count for two weeks.

With these two numbers, you can classify every item on your menu into one of four categories and make informed decisions about each one.

Menu engineering is not a one-time exercise. Run the analysis quarterly. Your best-seller in January may be a slow mover in July. Your highest-margin item may become unprofitable when ingredient costs rise. The menu should evolve based on evidence, not habit.

The goal is not to have the longest menu or the most creative one. It is to have a menu where every item earns its place — generating revenue, satisfying customers, or both.

sections.heading: The Star-Dog-Puzzle-Ploughorse Matrix
sections.body:
The menu engineering matrix classifies every item into four quadrants based on profitability and popularity.

**Stars — High profit, high popularity.**
These are your best items. They sell well and make you money. Protect them. Do not change the recipe. Do not raise the price aggressively. Position them prominently on the menu. Train the team to recommend them. Examples: your house flat white, a signature sandwich, a popular cake.

**Ploughorses — Low profit, high popularity.**
These sell well but do not make you much money. Customers love them, but the margin is thin. Your job is to improve profitability without killing demand. Options: reduce portion size slightly, find a cheaper supplier for key ingredients, increase the price by a small amount (twenty to thirty pence), or re-engineer the recipe to use lower-cost components. Do not remove ploughorses — they drive traffic.

**Puzzles — High profit, low popularity.**
These make good money when they sell, but they do not sell often enough. The question is: can you increase demand? Options: reposition them on the menu (move them to a more visible spot), rename them (a confusing name kills sales), have the team recommend them, offer them as a special. If demand does not improve after deliberate promotion, consider whether the item belongs on the menu at all.

**Dogs — Low profit, low popularity.**
These do not sell and do not make money when they do. Remove them. Every dog on your menu adds complexity to prep, takes up display space, creates potential waste, and distracts from your stars. Cafe owners resist removing items because "someone orders it sometimes." That is not a reason to keep it. If an item sells fewer than five times a week and has below-average margin, it is a dog. Cut it.

Use T039-01 to build your matrix. Plot every item. The visual makes the decisions obvious. Most cafes discover they have three or four dogs they have been carrying for years out of habit. Removing them simplifies operations, reduces waste, and focuses the team on what actually works.

sections.heading: Seasonal Specials That Drive Interest
sections.body:
A static menu is a stale menu. Seasonal specials keep regulars interested, give the team something new to talk about, and create social media moments that attract new customers.

**The rotation rhythm:**

Run seasonal specials on a four-to-six-week cycle. Long enough for customers to try them, short enough to maintain urgency. "Available until the end of March" creates a reason to visit this week rather than next month.

**What makes a good seasonal special:**

- **It uses seasonal ingredients.** A rhubarb and custard slice in spring, a pumpkin spice latte in autumn. Seasonal ingredients are often cheaper (because supply is high), taste better (because they are at their peak), and tell a story.
- **It is simple to execute.** A special that requires three extra ingredients, a new technique, and a custom garnish will slow down service and stress the team. The best specials use your existing setup with one or two twists.
- **It has a strong margin.** Specials command a premium. Customers expect to pay slightly more for something limited-edition. Price accordingly — aim for a higher contribution margin than your core menu items.
- **It photographs well.** Social media drives discovery for cafes. A visually distinctive special generates organic content when customers photograph and share it.

**Planning the calendar:**

Use T044-01 to map your seasonal specials for the year. Plan at least one season ahead so you can source ingredients, train the team, and prepare marketing.

| Season | Drink Special | Food Special | Launch Date | End Date |
|--------|--------------|-------------|-------------|----------|
| Spring | Lavender latte | Lemon & elderflower cake | 1 Mar | 15 Apr |
| Summer | Iced matcha | Strawberry scone | 1 Jun | 15 Jul |
| Autumn | Spiced apple chai | Fig & walnut toast | 15 Sep | 31 Oct |
| Winter | Gingerbread mocha | Mince pie brownie | 15 Nov | 31 Dec |

**Evaluating success:**

Track sales of each special weekly. If it is not selling after the first week, investigate: is the team recommending it? Is it visible on the menu board? Is the price right? If it still does not sell by week three, learn from it and move on. Not every special will be a hit, and that is fine.

sections.heading: Alternative Milks and Dietary Range
sections.body:
Alternative milks are no longer a niche request. Oat milk alone accounts for a significant and growing share of cafe milk orders. If you treat alternative milks as an inconvenience, you are telling a large portion of your potential customers that they are not welcome.

**Which milks to stock:**

- **Oat milk** is the default alternative for most cafes. It steams well, tastes neutral, and most barista-edition oat milks produce decent foam. Stock a barista-grade oat milk — the standard supermarket version splits and does not foam.
- **Soy milk** is the original alternative and still preferred by some customers. It curdles with acidic coffee, so pour gently and use a barista-grade soy that is designed for hot drinks.
- **Almond milk** produces thin foam and has a pronounced flavour. It is declining in popularity but some customers are loyal to it.
- **Coconut milk** is niche but growing. It adds a distinct flavour that works well with certain drinks (iced lattes, chai).

**Pricing:**

The industry trend is moving towards no surcharge for alternative milks. The cost difference between dairy and oat milk is typically ten to twenty pence per serving. Absorbing this cost removes a friction point and a perception of penalty. If your margins are tight, a small surcharge (thirty pence maximum) is defensible, but be aware that many competitors are dropping it.

Use T046-01 to calculate the actual cost impact of removing the surcharge across your expected volume. You may find it costs less than you think.

**Training:**

Every barista must know how to steam alternative milks properly. Each behaves differently — different aeration, different temperature tolerance, different foam density. A poorly steamed oat milk is as much a quality failure as a poorly steamed dairy milk. Include alternative milk technique in your barista training programme and your random quality checks.

**Dietary range beyond milk:**

Alternative milks are the visible part of dietary inclusivity, but the principle extends further. Do you have a genuinely good decaf option? Can you serve a satisfying drink to someone who does not drink coffee at all? Is there a food option for someone who is vegan, gluten-free, or both? Every gap in your range is a customer turned away.

sections.heading: Batch Brew and Filter Programmes
sections.body:
Batch brew is the most underrated revenue opportunity in most cafes. It is faster than espresso, cheaper to produce, requires less barista skill, and — for a growing segment of customers — it is the preferred way to drink coffee.

**Why batch brew makes business sense:**

- **Speed.** Pouring a batch brew takes ten seconds. Making an espresso-based drink takes sixty to ninety seconds. During a rush, batch brew is your pressure valve.
- **Cost.** A litre of batch brew costs roughly one pound fifty in coffee. It yields four to five cups at three pounds each. That is a margin north of seventy-five percent.
- **Consistency.** A well-dialled batch brew recipe produces the same result every time. There is no tamping variation, no distribution issues, no steaming errors.
- **Customer preference.** Filter coffee drinkers are a loyal and growing segment. They tend to be knowledgeable about coffee, appreciate variety, and are willing to pay for quality.

**Setting up a batch brew programme:**

- **Equipment.** A commercial batch brewer (Moccamaster, Fetco, or Curtis) costs between three hundred and eight hundred pounds. It pays for itself within weeks.
- **Recipe.** Start with a 1:16 ratio (60g coffee per litre of water). Adjust by taste. Use a refractometer if you have one — target 1.2–1.4% TDS for a balanced brew.
- **Rotation.** Brew in small batches and hold for a maximum of thirty to forty-five minutes. Stale batch brew is worse than no batch brew. Time-stamp every batch.
- **Variety.** Rotate your batch brew offering. Use it to showcase single-origins, guest roasters, or experimental lots. "Today's filter: Kenyan Nyeri, red fruit, bright acidity" gives customers a reason to try something different each visit.

**How to sell it:**

Batch brew sells itself if you position it correctly. Offer a taster to curious customers. Display the coffee bag with tasting notes. Train the team to talk about it with enthusiasm, not apology. Too many cafes treat batch brew as the budget option. Frame it as the craft option — a different way to experience great coffee, not a cheaper alternative to espresso.

sections.heading: The Cold Drink Opportunity
sections.body:
Cold drinks are where cafes leave the most money on the table. Summers are getting warmer, and even in winter, a significant number of customers prefer iced drinks. If your cold menu is an afterthought — an iced latte and a can of Coke — you are missing a major revenue stream.

**Cold coffee:**

- **Iced latte.** The baseline. Espresso over ice with cold milk. Simple, fast, popular. But differentiate it — use a specific recipe (espresso poured over ice first, then milk) rather than letting each barista improvise.
- **Cold brew.** Steep coarse-ground coffee in cold water for twelve to eighteen hours. Strain and store in the fridge. It keeps for up to a week and is ready to pour. Cold brew has a smooth, low-acid profile that appeals to customers who find iced espresso too intense.
- **Iced filter.** Flash-brew: brew hot coffee directly over ice using a pour-over or batch method. It preserves the brightness and complexity of the coffee, which cold brew smooths out.

**Non-coffee cold drinks:**

- **Iced matcha and chai.** High-margin, visually appealing, popular on social media. Use quality matcha (ceremonial or barista grade) and make your chai in-house if possible.
- **Fresh juices and smoothies.** Only if you have the equipment and the volume to justify it. Fresh juice requires a commercial juicer, generates significant waste, and must be served immediately. Smoothies require a blender and prep space. Both are labour-intensive.
- **Lemonade and iced tea.** House-made lemonade and iced tea are simple, cheap to produce, and highly profitable. A batch of lemonade costs pennies per glass and sells for two to three pounds.

**Use T049-01 to plan your cold drink programme:**

Map each drink against: ingredient cost, preparation time, equipment required, shelf life, and expected demand. Start with three or four items and expand based on sales data.

**Merchandising cold drinks:**

Cold drinks sell on sight. A glass-fronted fridge stocked with colourful bottles of cold brew, lemonade, and iced tea near the counter is more effective than any menu board description. If customers can see it, they buy it. In summer, position your cold display prominently and train the team to suggest cold options when the weather is warm.
